mechanism_narrative: Sustained month-over-month growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) indicates a strengthening economic foundation, leading to upward revisions in corporate earnings expectations and driving positive equity returns over the medium term.
evidence_refs: fact:Labor market health is a primary indicator of economic cycle stage.
failure_condition: The 3-month change in PAYEMS shows a negative or statistically insignificant correlation with 60-day forward S&P 500 returns over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate (UNRATE) is extremely high, as it is now post-GFC, it signals to markets that the Federal Reserve will maintain an aggressively accommodative monetary policy (low FEDFUNDS) for an extended period, which supports equity valuations.
evidence_refs: fact:Current UNRATE is 10.0%, a multi-decade high.
failure_condition: When UNRATE is above 8.0%, the average 60-day forward S&P 500 return is not statistically higher than periods when UNRATE is below 8.0%.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp contraction in housing starts (HOUST) is a leading indicator of a severe downturn in consumer and business investment, negatively impacting future GDP growth and corporate profitability, thus leading to negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:The 2008 crisis was precipitated by a housing market collapse.
failure_condition: Instances where HOUST falls more than 20% year-over-year are not followed by negative S&P 500 returns on average over the subsequent 60 days.

mechanism_narrative: In a post-recession environment, a positive inflection in the rate of change of industrial production (INDPRO) signals that the supply side of the economy is recovering, leading to improved operating leverage, positive earnings surprises, and higher equity prices.
evidence_refs: fact:Current context is recovery from the 2008 Global Financial Crisis.
failure_condition: Positive shocks to INDPRO during the post-2008 recovery period fail to predict positive 20-day S&P 500 returns in more than 50% of triggered instances.

mechanism_narrative: With the Federal Funds rate at the zero lower bound, any unexpected increase in inflation (CPIAUCSL) reduces real rates further. This 'financial repression' forces capital into risk assets like equities to seek positive real returns, boosting the S&P 500.
failure_condition: During the ZIRP era, the correlation between monthly changes in CPIAUCSL and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is not statistically positive.

mechanism_narrative: An elevated and rising unemployment rate (UNRATE) directly signals weakness in aggregate demand and consumer confidence. This leads to lower revenue forecasts for consumer-facing companies, causing negative revisions and equity market declines.
evidence_refs: fact:UNRATE is at a cycle high of 10.0%.
failure_condition: The level of the unemployment rate fails to exhibit a statistically significant negative correlation with 20-day forward S&P 500 returns over a 10-year period.

mechanism_narrative: A deceleration in retail sales (RSAFS) growth is a high-frequency indicator of weakening consumer demand, which constitutes the largest part of the US economy. This slowdown leads to immediate downward revisions of near-term earnings estimates, causing negative equity returns.
failure_condition: Periods of retail sales growth below 1% are not followed by a statistically significant underperformance of the S&P 500 over the next 20 days.

mechanism_narrative: Unexpectedly high monthly inflation prints (CPIAUCSL) create short-term uncertainty about the future path of monetary policy, even with rates at zero. This increases the equity risk premium and causes a negative market reaction over a very short horizon.
failure_condition: The average 5-day S&P 500 return following CPI prints above 0.4% m/m is not statistically lower than the average return following prints below this threshold.
